coredata.dc:title: Poly glycerol sebacate/ polycaprolactone/ carbon quantum dots fibrous scaffold as a multifunctional platform for cardiac tissue engineering
coredata.dc:creator: Rastegar, Sara
Mehdikhani, Mehdi
Bigham, Ashkan
Poorazizi, Elahe
Rafienia, Mohammad
coredata.dc:description: In this study, a new nanocomposite scaffold entailing poly glycerol sebacate/polycaprolactone/carbon quantum dots (PGS/PCL/CQDs) was designed and fabricated for cardiac muscle regeneration. The PGS/PCL fibrous scaffolds were electrospun in disparate weight ratios—2:1 and 1:1. Next, different amounts of CQDs (0.5 and 1 wt %) were incorporated in the PGS/PCL fibers to reach a ternary nanocomposite scaffold. Besides characterizing the physical and chemical properties of scaffolds—morphology, chemical bonds, mechanical properties, wettability, and electrical conductivity—the biological properties including cytotoxicity, cell attachment and proliferation, and degradation rate were assessed in vitro. To give proof of CQDs inclusion in the fibers, transmission electron microscopy and fluorescent assay were applied. Addition of both PGS and CQDs to the PCL fibers resulted in a significant decrease in the mean fiber diameter of ternary nanocomposite scaffold from 862 ± 167 down to 376.82 ± 150 nm. The electrical conductivity of scaffolds was increased through the incorporation of CQDs, whereas the addition of CQDs up to 1 wt % led to a decrease in the cell viability. According to the weight ratio optimization, the PGS/PCL/CQDs scaffold (2:1:0.5) outperformed the others in physical, chemical, and biological properties; the scaffold's Young's modulus, elongation at break, and ultimate tensile strength were 11 ± 1 MPa, 10 ± 1 mm, and 5 ± 1 MPa, respectively. Moreover, the cell viability of optimized nanocomposite scaffold was found to be very close to the negative control proving its desirable cell compatibility. Therefore, the optimized PGS/PCL/CQDs nanocomposite scaffold can be potentially promising for cardiac muscle tissue engineering.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanofibrous scaffold
Cardiac muscle
Poly glycerol sebacate
Polycaprolactone
Carbon quantum dots

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: University of Isfahan and Isfahan University of Medical Sciences

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the scientific support for this work by the University of Isfahan and Isfahan University of Medical Sciences.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the CQDs.
The particle size distribution of CQDs dispersed in water.
The SEM micrographs of electrospun scaffolds: (A) P, (B) G1P1, and (C) G2P1 (scale bar 10 μm).
The SEM micrographs of electrospun scaffolds: (A) PQ0.5, (B) G1P1Q0.5, (C) G2P1Q0.5, (D) PQ1, (E) G1P1Q1, and (F) G2P1Q1 (scale bar 10 μm).
Orientation assessment and distribution of all scaffolds: (A,a) P, (B,b) G1P1, (C,c) G2P1, (D,d) PQ0.5, (E,e) G1P1Q0.5, (F,f) G1P1Q1, (G,g) PQ1, (H,h) G2P1Q0.5, (I,i) G2P1Q1.
The fluorescence microscopy micrographs of scaffolds (A) PQ0.5, (B) G1P1Q0.5, (C) G2P1Q0.5, (D) PQ1, (E) G1P1Q1, (F) G2P1Q1, (G) P.
The TEM micrographs of (A) PQ0.5, (B) G2P1Q0.5, and (C) G2P1Q1 nanocomposite scaffolds; scale bar 300 nm.
The FTIR spectra of (A) G, (B) Q, (C) P, and (D) G2/P1/Q0.5.
The wettability measurements of nanocomposite scaffolds after 20 s.
The water uptake (%) capability of scaffolds up to 72 h.
In vitro degradation rate of scaffolds in PBS up to 60 days.
The SEM micrographs related to the rats' myoblast cells attachment on the scaffolds up to 3 days; (A) P, (B) PQ0.5, (C) G2P1, (D) G1P1Q1, (E) G2P1Q0.5, and (F) G2P1Q1.

The DAPI staining (blue: nucleus) micrographs of the rat's myoblast cells cultured on (A) P, (B) PQ0.5, (C) G2P1, (D) G1P1Q1, (E) G2P1Q0.5, and (F) G2P1Q1 scaffolds up to 14 days; scale bar at 300 nm. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
The cell viability of electrospun scaffolds up to 7 days; *p > 0.05,**. *p ≤ 0.05.
The mean fiber diameter and porosity of scaffolds.
Mechanical properties of the scaffolds in dry and wet conditions.

head.ce:title: Poly glycerol sebacate/ polycaprolactone/ carbon quantum dots fibrous scaffold as a multifunctional platform for cardiac tissue engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a new nanocomposite scaffold entailing poly glycerol sebacate/polycaprolactone/carbon quantum dots (PGS/PCL/CQDs) was designed and fabricated for cardiac muscle regeneration. The PGS/PCL fibrous scaffolds were electrospun in disparate weight ratios—2:1 and 1:1. Next, different amounts of CQDs (0.5 and 1 wt %) were incorporated in the PGS/PCL fibers to reach a ternary nanocomposite scaffold. Besides characterizing the physical and chemical properties of scaffolds—morphology, chemical bonds, mechanical properties, wettability, and electrical conductivity—the biological properties including cytotoxicity, cell attachment and proliferation, and degradation rate were assessed in vitro. To give proof of CQDs inclusion in the fibers, transmission electron microscopy and fluorescent assay were applied. Addition of both PGS and CQDs to the PCL fibers resulted in a significant decrease in the mean fiber diameter of ternary nanocomposite scaffold from 862 ± 167 down to 376.82 ± 150 nm. The electrical conductivity of scaffolds was increased through the incorporation of CQDs, whereas the addition of CQDs up to 1 wt % led to a decrease in the cell viability. According to the weight ratio optimization, the PGS/PCL/CQDs scaffold (2:1:0.5) outperformed the others in physical, chemical, and biological properties; the scaffold's Young's modulus, elongation at break, and ultimate tensile strength were 11 ± 1 MPa, 10 ± 1 mm, and 5 ± 1 MPa, respectively. Moreover, the cell viability of optimized nanocomposite scaffold was found to be very close to the negative control proving its desirable cell compatibility. Therefore, the optimized PGS/PCL/CQDs nanocomposite scaffold can be potentially promising for cardiac muscle tissue engineering.

ce:section.ce:para: Despite enormous progress in cardiac tissue engineering, heart-related diseases still take victims around the world. Among heart-related problems, more people fall victim to myocardial infarction (MI) leading to congestive heart failure (CHF). The CHF refers to a situation in which supplying of blood through human's body is hampered due to the incapability of heart [1]. The heart's muscle tissue does not have the ability to repair and restore the damaged area and so the survived people from MI would experience another grave condition like formation of scar tissue and other issues which again result in CHF [2].

As it is obvious, organ donors are in short supply and that is the reason why medical practitioners are looking for a reliable solution to address the cardiac tissue problems effectively [3]. Tissue engineering as a multidisciplinary field of study is considered as an area which integrates medicine, biology, and engineering and so new and effective solutions are introduced to the issues which have threatened humans survival for a long time [4]. The three main characteristics of native myocardium making up a set of cardiac tissue engineering design requirements are as follows: (i) presence of myocytes cells in a high density, (ii) existence of an appropriate oxygen exchange between both blood and the cells, and (iii) the simultaneous contractions which can be provided through electrical signals [5]. Therefore, design and fabrication of a functional scaffold resembling the structural and mechanical properties of native myocardium require an inordinate amount of effort [6].

It is well known that biomaterials are of crucial importance when it comes to tissue engineering; selection of an appropriate biomaterial based on the desired application equals to the successful regeneration of damaged tissue followed by acquiring its natural condition again [7]. Biopolymers are regarded as a suitable choice to mimic myocardium structure, but before being applied into cardiac tissue engineering, they must meet some basic requirements—suitable degradability, biocompatibility, and desirable physical properties close to the host tissue [8]. Hence, designing a compound capable of fulfilling the necessities of myocardial regeneration is of great significance.

As a synthetic biopolymer, poly glycerol sebacate (PGS) has been at the epicenter of researchers’ attention rooting in its incredible properties—tailorable physical and chemical properties, and great biological response after being in contact with living cells [9]. In 2002, PGS was introduced and applied in soft tissue regeneration [10]; it was found that the cell compatibility—cell viability, adhesion, and proliferation—was significantly improved when PGS had been applied both in vitro and in vivo. A great effort has been made to alter the PGS elastic modulus for cardiac tissue regeneration [11], but due to its low molecular weight, fabrication of a scaffold made of sole PGS was a tough challenge. Therefore, some researchers have come up with designing PGS scaffolds in combination with other polymers—natural and/or synthetic—through electrospinning [11,12].

One of the most well-known biopolymers in tissue engineering is polycaprolactone (PCL) which has a semi-crystalline structure; this polymer has gained its reputation because of many advantages—biocompatibility, great physical properties, chemical stability, etc [13–15]. Therefore, combination of PCL with PGS would compensate the PGS drawbacks culminating in a functional hybrid for cardiac tissue regeneration.

Electro-conductive scaffolds are of great importance in cardiac muscle tissue development because they belong to a family known as smart biomaterials; these materials are naturally electroactive that means the cellular activity would be dramatically enhanced once electrochemical stimulations are applied [16]. Different kinds of materials are considered electroactive among which conductive ceramics, polymers, and piezoelectric biomaterials can be enumerated [17].

One of the most attractive subcategories of carbon materials is carbon quantum dots (CQDs) having some extraordinary characteristics—biocompatibility, electrical conductivity, thermal stability, photosensitivity, etc.—by which they stand out in the carbon materials family. The mentioned properties beside good solubility and ease of being functionalized by different organic groups have drawn a great attention to apply CQDs in biomedical engineering [18]. In the case of cancer therapy, after administration of drug-loaded CQDs into blood stream, they become highly dispersive and because of their particle size (˂10 nm) it is much easier to penetrate through tumors structure. Moreover, their incredible luminescence nature gives ample opportunity to track them (biolabling) and see if CQDs have reached their destination successfully [19]. Speaking of cardiovascular-related applications, when a patient suffers from MI, the signaling of electrical pulses would be hampered in infarcted areas and so an electroconductive biomaterial like carbon materials is able to introduce an effective solution and regenerate the cardiac tissue after being implanted in the region [3]. Crowder et al. [20] have designed an electroconductive scaffold composed of PCL/carbon nanotubes to assess whether mesenchymal stem cells can experience cardiomyogenic differentiation and the results showed that the electrical stimulation has led to an accelerated differentiation in the stem cells.

Disparate techniques have been adopted so far to construct porous scaffolds with various morphologies and porosity accounting for different physical, chemical, and biological properties [21,22]. As it is known the extra cellular matrix of myocardium can be mimicked well through electrospinning technique and many researchers in the field have applied it to design and fabricate porous scaffolds for cardiac tissue regeneration. It is noteworthy that electrospinning technique provides some features to scaffolds on the upside making it even more attractive for soft tissue regeneration—high surface area, various morphological properties based on the desired application, desirable mechanical properties [23].

In the present study, a new multifunctional nanocomposite scaffold composed of PGS/PCL/CQDs was designed and fabricated through electrospinning technique for cardiac tissue regeneration. The scaffold was step by step undergone different characterization techniques and tests to shed light on the effects of each composition on the physical, chemical, and biological properties of the ternary nanocomposite scaffold.

PCL (Mw = 80000) and HPLC water were provided from Sigma Aldrich (USA). Glycerol (99%), sebacic acid (99%), 1,1,1,3,3,3, hexafluoro-2-propanol (HFIP), 2-hydroxypropane-1,2,3-tricarboxylic acid, and dimethylamine were purchased from Merck (Germany). l-glutamine, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazoliu bromide (MTT), N,N-dimethylformamide, fetal bovine serum (FBS), and trypsin–ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) were obtained from Bio Idea (India). Mouse myoblasts cells were provided from Pasteur Institute of Iran.

The CQDs were synthesized through polycondensation reaction [18,24]. Briefly, 1.0546 g of 2-hydroxypropane-1,2,3-tricarboxylic acid, and 335 μL dimethylamine were first mixed. Next, 10 mL of HPLC water was added and the mixture was placed on a magnetic stirrer to become uniform. It should be noted that an aluminum foil wrapped around the container did not allow any light to reach the solution. Afterwards, the resulting solution was transferred to a sterile autoclave and heated for 5 h at 200 °C. After 5 h, the autoclave cell was given the opportunity to cool. After cooling the solution, it was centrifuged at 7000 rpm followed by a filtration process. Finally, the supernatant was filtered through a microporous membrane (0.2 μm), and then dried in a vacuum oven at 37 °C for 24 h. The CQDs were characterized through dynamic light scattering (DLS, SZ-100z, Horiba Jobin Jyovin, Germany), UV–Vis spectrophotometer (OPTIZEN 3220UV, South Korea), and Fourier transform-infrared (FTIR, JASCO, FT/IR-6300, Japan).

In this research, the PGS was synthesized through the polycondensation reaction which was previously reported by our group [12]. Briefly, a 1:0.8 M mixture of glycerol and sebacic acid was reacted in a round bottom flask with two necks at 170 °C under nitrogen gas for 3 h. FTIR was applied to characterize the functional groups of PGS. To fabricate the ternary nanocomposite scaffold through electrospinning technique, preparation of a uniform solution is inevitable. First, a PCL solution was prepared through dissolving the polymer (12 wt %) into HFIP on a magnetic stirrer (500 rpm) for 12 h. Next, two weight ratios (PGS/PCL: 2:1 and 1:1) for the combination of PGS and PCL were used and 2 h were given to the solution to become uniform and clear. The CQDs with disparate weight ratios (0.5 and 1 wt %) were then added to the PGS/PCL solution followed by exposing to sonication for 10 min. The fabrication parameters by which the scaffolds were electrospun were as follows: a 23 G needle, a flow rate of 0.6 mL/h, a voltage of 15 kV, and a distance of 25 cm between the collector and tip. The whole fabrication procedure was accomplished at room temperature followed by drying the scaffolds into a vacuum oven at 30 °C for 24 h. It is worth mentioning that the fibrous scaffolds with aligned fibers structure were collected through a rotated collector when the vertical and horizontal speeds had been set on 2000 and 100 rpm in turn. Table 1 summarizes the coded samples with different weight ratios.

To analyze the physical and chemical properties of fibrous scaffolds, various characterization techniques were applied as follows: FTIR, scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Philips-FEI Quanta 200, USA), transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Philips EM208S 100 KV), and fluorescent microscopy (Nikon Eclipse Ti, japan). ImageJ software was adopted to measure the average fiber diameter, porosity, and orientation distribution of fibrous scaffolds. To determine the fibers’ mean angle, 100 fibers were chosen approximately through the SEM micrographs followed by measuring the angle between the fibers and the grid line.

The wettability of prepared scaffolds was measured through dropping 4 μL distilled water on each scaffold followed by determining the formed angle between the surface of scaffolds and the droplet through a contact angle meter (FACE-CA-X50, USA). The results regarding to the wettability of each sample were based on the average of three measurements.

The water absorption capacity of scaffolds was assessed through submerging each sample (10 × 10 mm2) into 5 mL of phosphate buffered saline (PBS) followed by preserving them in an incubator at 37 °C up to 72 h. At each specific time interval, the scaffolds were first taken out of PBS containers and then the excess of PBS from the surface of samples were deftly removed. The water uptake percentage was calculated through Eq. (1):
(1)
S
( % )
=
Wt − Wd
Wd
× 100
where Wt is the scaffolds' weight after being submerged into PBS and Wd is the samples' weight after drying.

Following ASTM-F1635 protocols, the scaffolds’ degradation rate was assessed up to 60 days through immersing each sample into 20 mL of PBS followed by incubation at 37 °C. The samples were removed at intended time intervals, rinsed with distilled water, and dried in an oven for 24 h. The degradation rate was calculated through Eq. (2) as follows:
(2)
Δ W
( % )
=
W 0 − Wd
W 0
× 100
where W0 is considered as the samples' weight before being immersed into PBS and Wd is the dried samples' weight after being soaked into PBS and taken out at the specific time interval. It is noteworthy that the degradation rate of each sample was an average of three identical samples.

The tension test (TM–SM, Instron, England) was adopted to measure the mechanical properties of electrospun scaffolds—Young's modulus, ultimate tensile strength (UTS), and elongation at break. The mechanical test parameters including the load cell, the applied speed rate, and the scaffolds' shape were based on ASTEM-D638 standard. To simulate in vivo conditions, the scaffolds' mechanical properties were accomplished in both dry and wet (after being soaked into PBS) conditions. The mechanical test results were based on the average of three similar scaffolds.

The method by which the electrical conductivity of electrospun scaffolds was measured, is the four-point-probe method [26]. For this purpose, the scaffolds were electrospun one by one on a glass substrate and then they were kept in an oven over a night at 37 °C to be completely dried. A Keithley instrument (Model 196 System DMM, USA) was adopted to determine the scaffolds' electrical conductivity. A constant current was applied through other probes in order to determine the scaffolds’ sheet resistivity and Eq. (3) was used to calculate the bulk resistivity of scaffolds as follows:
(3)
ρ =
π
ln
2
V
( I )
t
where V, t, and I are the voltage, the scaffolds' thickness (200 μm), and the applied current, respectively. As the resistivity has an inverse relationship with the electrical conductivity, it was obtained based on the resistivity value. The results were based on an average of testing three similar samples.

At first, mouse myoblast cells were exposed to a Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM) containing penicillin/streptomycin (5 mL) and fetal bovine serum (FBS) followed by being preserved in an incubator (37 °C and 5% CO2). The medium was regularly refreshed and once a confluence had been reached, the rinsing and separation of cells through PBS and Trypsin-EDTA were occurred in turn. To sterilize the samples, they were exposed to UV light for 2 h and after being in direct contact with the cells for 1 and 3 days, the samples were rinsed trice with PBS. In order to fix the cells on the surface of scaffolds for the cell attachment study, glutaraldehyde (3%) was added and the medium was kept for 2 h at 4 °C. Next, dehydration process through using ethanol from 50 to 100 wt % was accomplished and finally, the scaffolds were taken to be assessed by SEM.

The cell viability of electrospun scaffolds were assessed through MTT assay up to 7 days. At each specific time interval—1, 3, and 7 days—the DMEM was removed followed by rinsing the scaffolds with sterile PBS. Next, 40 μL of MTT solution (0.5 mg/mL) was added to the medium and the scaffolds were then preserved for 4 h in an incubator with the mentioned conditions. The MTT solution was wiped out and DMSO (100 μL) was added to the medium and kept for an additional 2 h. Eventually, the medium was taken to be assessed with an ELISA reader at the wavelength of 540 nm.

In the present study, DAPI was used to determine the attached cell's nucleus clearly after 7 and 14 days. For this purpose, the fixation of cells was carried out through keeping the cells in the exposure of 4% paraformaldehyde for 10 min followed by rinsing with PBS and addition of 0.4% Triton X‐100. After 30 min being in the exposure of Triton X-100, rinsing with PBS was accomplished and DAPI (0.5 μg/mL) was added. The scaffolds' surface were then observed with a fluorescent image analyzer (Nikon Eclipse Ti, Japan).

The reported results in the present study were based on mean ± standard deviation (SD). There were three identical samples used for each test and the independent one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) was adopted here to analyze the statistical differences between the samples through the SPSS software (P ≤ 0.05).

The FTIR spectrum of CQDs is indicated through Fig. 1 , where the bands in the range of 3200–3500 cm−1 and 2800-2900 cm−1 were pertained to C–H and O–H stretching bonding, respectively, indicating oxidation at the surface of CQDs has occurred. Moreover, the peaks of N–H appeared in the zone from 3000 to 3300 cm−1. The two bands relating to CC and CO were observable at 1400 and 1600 cm−1, respectively. The C–O band at 1100 cm−1 was attributed to hydroxyl and ester groups. The results in this section imply that the synthesized CQDs were successfully obtained and the FTIR spectrum is in good agreement with previous studies [18,27].

UV–Vis spectroscopy was performed to investigate the absorption property of CQDs. The absorbance spectrum of CQDs exhibited two peaks—an intense peak centered at 220 nm, and another one at 350 nm (Fig. 2 ).

In order to evaluate the hydrodynamic size of nanoparticles, DLS test was applied. According to Fig. 3 , the average particle size of CQDs was 6.5 nm. The presence of only one peak in the DLS diagram indicates that the nanoparticles were monodisperse. The performed analyses related to CQDs here implied the successful synthesis of nanoparticles and they are consistent with the results of Dager et al. [24].

Fabrication of electrospun scaffolds to reach successful myocardium regeneration needs some preliminary requirements to be met in the first stage like electrospinnability of the composition. The high potential of PGS for myocardium regeneration is not obscure to the scientific community, but the electrospinning of PGS solely is challenging stemming from its low viscosity and molecular weight accounting for addition of another polymer to compensate this weakness [27]. PCL is considered as an appropriate candidate to be combined with PGS because of its great physical, chemical, and biological properties [15]. Herein, the scaffolds with different weight ratios were fabricated through electrospinning technique to assess the effects of each composition on the physical, chemical, and biological properties of scaffolds. It is important to mention that the fibrous scaffolds were only obtained when the weight ratios were 2:1 and 1:1 for PGS:PCL. According to the applied electrospinning parameters—the total polymer concentration (18% w/v), the flow rate (0.5 mL/h), distance between the collector and needle (25 cm), and the applied voltage (15 kV)—the polymeric solutions with higher PGS ratios could not be electrospun. It is noteworthy that the prepared electrospun scaffolds with different weight ratios are called based on their code (Table 1). Fig. 4 (A-C) indicates the SEM micrographs of P, G1P1, and G2P1; it is clear that the obtained fibers were well aligned and no bead was observable. The next step was the addition of CQDs to the PGS/PCL fibrous scaffolds; Fig. 5 exhibits the SEM micrographs of PQ0.5, G1P1Q0.5, G2P1Q0.5, PQ1, G1P1Q1, and G2P1Q1 scaffolds and through Table 2 , the porosity and mean fiber diameter of all scaffolds were summarized. The mean fiber diameter of P, G1P1, and G2P1 were 862 ± 167, 604.50 ± 191, and 493.65 ± 151 nm, respectively. The consecutive decrease in the diameter after addition of PGS to PCL pertained to the effects of PGS on the solution—a decrease in both viscosity and entanglement of polymer chains [25]. The reduction in fiber diameter was also continued after the addition of CQDs (0.5 and 1 wt %) to P, G1P1, and G2P1 scaffolds and the maximum decrement (376 ± 150 nm) was obtained when the weight ratios of PGS:PCL were increased to 2:1 and the CQDs in 1 wt % were added. The total porosity (%) was found to have a reverse relationship with the fibers diameter and the scaffolds’ porosity were in the range of 50–73%.

ImageJ software was adopted here to assess the scaffolds’ fiber alignment and through Fig. 6 , the fibers alignment and their distribution of orientation are exhibited. As can be seen, there is mainly a difference between the scaffolds with and without PGS; in the case of pure PCL fibers, a random orientation is visible and even the addition of CQDs to the PCL scaffolds did not significantly affect the orientation in a positive manner. However, combination of PGS with PCL was synchronized with a significant increase in the PCL/PGS scaffolds evidencing the effect of PGS to make the fibers aligned. Therefore, it can be deduced that the fiber alignment is directly related to the increasing in the PGS contents.

To give proof of CQDs distribution all over the fibers, the scaffolds containing CQDs were exposed to UV through the fluorescence microscopy. Fig. 7 (A-G) shows the fluorescent images of the fibers—PQ0.5 (A), G1P1Q0.5 (B), G2P1Q0.5 (C), PQ1 (D), G1P1Q1 (E), G2P1Q1 (F), and PCL (G). Increasing in CQDs content was accompanied with an increase in the emission rate obviously. It is worth mentioning that despite increasing in the amounts of PGS, the fluorescence property of CQDs in the scaffolds' structure was maintained proving PGS had no effect on this feature. To compare, the PCL scaffold's fluorescence property was examined and a completely black image showing no emission in the response to UV was obtained. TEM was also applied to examine the fibers containing CQDs and the micrographs of PQ0.5, G2P1Q0.5, and G2P1Q1 are indicated through Fig. 8 . Through the previous section, it was clearly shown that the CQDs were homogenously distributed all over the fibers, but in the case of TEM micrographs, some agglomerations are visible through the fibers, but they were not too much to affect the fibers' structure negatively.

Through Fig. 9 , the FTIR patterns of PGS (A), CQDs (B), PCL (C), and G2P1Q0.5 (D) are shown, where the PGS functional groups including methylene (-CH2), C–O, and formation of CO have shown themselves at 2858–2931, 1182, and 1734 cm−1, respectively. Moreover, the OH groups—the bending and stretching vibrations—were appeared at 1510 and 3460 cm−1 in turn [12,25]. In the case of PCL, different peaks were appeared in three areas—1100-1300, 1700, and 2800-2950 cm−1; in the very first range (1100-1300 cm−1), symmetric (1161 cm−1) and asymmetric (1292 cm−1) stretching bands of C–O were located and the bands at 1726 and 2800-2950 cm−1 pertained to the carbonyl groups and CH2 vibrations, respectively [13,14]. The FTIR pattern of scaffold (G2P1Q0.5) contains the peaks belonging to PCL, PGS, and CQDs all of which comply that the ternary composite was obtained. It is important to mention that due to overlapping, some peaks could not clearly show themselves.

The wettability of all scaffolds was assessed to see if the addition of each composition—PGS and CQDs—to the PCL had an effect on its hydrophobicity. It is important to notice that the wettability of a scaffold is a pivotal criterion by which the cells attachment and proliferation through the scaffold's internal structure can be affected and so its assessment is of great concern when it comes to designing a platform for tissue engineering [28,29]. The results are indicated through Fig. 10 , where the contact angle of pure PCL scaffold was 137 ± 16° indicating its hydrophobic nature. The combination of PGS with PCL caused the contact angle to plummet below 20°. Due to the high hydrophilicy potential of PGS/PCL scaffolds, the addition of CQDs to them did not significantly affect the wettability despite the fact that the addition of CQDs to pure PCL led to a slight decrease in its hydrophobicity.

The hydrophilic nature of PGS and its tendency to absorb wide range of water molecules is well known and that was the reason why the wettability of pure PCL scaffolds was enhanced significantly [17]. Speaking of cells permeation and proliferation through a scaffold, there are some governing parameters—surface roughness, hydrophilicity, composition, and surface charge [30,31]. In this study, it was attempted to establish a reasonable equilibrium between PCL, PGS, and CQDs to modify the wettability of scaffolds in a way in which a proper cell attachment and diffusion through the scaffolds’ structure can be reached.

Fig. 11 shows the water uptake trend of all scaffolds after immersion into PBS at 37 °C up to 72 h. Despite taking the type of scaffold into account, all samples experienced a sharp increase in the water uptake during first 3 h of soaking followed by having a steady trend up to 48 h. It is noteworthy that the ternary scaffolds containing PGS/PCL/CQDs observed a drop in their water uptake trend after 48 h, up to 72 h, which may have rooted in the gradual degradation of these scaffolds; due to their hydrophilic nature, they were capable of taking up more water and so more corrosion.

Generally, the water uptake increased when PGS/PCL ratio rose. Moreover, the addition of CQDs to both pure PCL and PGS/PCL scaffolds has also led to an increase in their water uptake, but the effect of PGS on the water uptake was stronger than the CQDs as mentioned before which can be ascribed to its strong hydrophilic nature.

The in vitro degradability of P, G1P1, G2P1, PQ0.5, G1P1Q0.5, G2P1Q0.5, PQ1, G1P1Q1, and G2P1Q1 nanofibrous scaffolds was assessed through submerging them into PBS up to 60 days at 37 °C. As it is shown in Fig. 12 , the degradation rate of PGS/PCL and ternary scaffolds was significantly more and faster than the pure PCL and PCL/CQDs scaffolds (P ≤ 0.05). The pure PCL and PCL/CQDs scaffolds have seen a slight increase in their degradation rate and the final weight loss (%) was below 10% for all of them, whereas the other scaffolds have experienced a consecutive raise in their numbers till the end. By the end of period, the G2P1Q0.5 and G2P1Q1 scaffolds had lost nearly 50% of their initial weights. The main reason behind this phenomenon was attributed to the effect of PGS and CQDs on the wettability of ternary scaffolds accounting for more water absorption and faster degradation rate compared to the pure PCL and PCL/CQDs scaffolds.

Besides meeting biological requisites—biocompatibility and biodegradability—the scaffold's mechanical properties should be suitable based on its potential application [25,32]. In both dry and wet conditions, the scaffolds Young's modulus, UTS, and elongation at break were assessed and the results were summarized in Table 3 . According to Table 3, as the result of raising the PGS contents, the Young's modulus in dry and wet conditions increased from 1 ± 0.2 (P) to 8 ± 0.5 (G2P1) MPa and 0.72 ± 0.3 (P) to 6 ± 0.6 MPa (G2P1), respectively. Moreover, the addition of CQDs to the PGS/PCL scaffolds was accompanied with even further increase in the Young's Modulus up to 13 ± 1 MPa.

It was demonstrated that the cardiac muscle Young's modulus is in the range of 10 KPa up to 0.5 MPa [27,33]. Based on the obtained results here, the ternary scaffolds mechanical properties are in the acceptable reported range. Nevertheless, the Young's modulus was significantly different for dry and wet conditions (P < 0.05). It may be related to either the water absorption of scaffolds containing PGS after soaking into PBS for 24 h or the release of CQDs from the fibers leading to the reduction of Young's modulus of scaffolds in wet condition. Despite the decrease in Young's modulus values when it comes to wet conditions, the mechanical properties are still considered acceptable and in the range of cardiac tissue. On the contrary, the UTS of scaffolds was increased when the test had been taken in wet condition. The reason why this phenomenon was occurred ascribed to the fiber fusion which was reported by Saudi et al. [12] as well.

Table 4 contains the data related to electrical conductivity of scaffolds. Based on Table 4, there was a direct relationship between the addition of CQDs to the scaffolds and their electrical conductivity as it is well known that carbon materials and its derivatives are electrically conductive [34]. It was found that the scaffolds containing both PGS and CQDs had an electrical conductivity in the range of 0.40–0.56 S/m.

The cell attachment study was applied to assess if the addition of each composition to PCL scaffold would affect the cells morphology on the scaffolds. Fig. 13 exhibits the SEM micrographs of the rats’ myoblasts cells on P, PQ0.5, G2P1, G1P1Q1, G2P1Q0.5, and G2P1Q1 scaffolds at different time intervals—1 and 3 days. It is clear that after 1 day, some cells were attached on the surface of scaffolds. In the case of PCL and PCL/CQDs scaffolds, the adhered cells were spherical in morphology, whereas the cells attached on the other scaffolds composed of PGS/PCL/CQDs were membrane-like which might have rooted in the wettability of scaffolds. Continuing the cell attachment study up to 3 days revealed that the cells density was increased evidencing the high cell compatible nature of scaffolds; it seems that a complete matrix was formed on the surface of ternary scaffolds. Possibly, the addition of CQDs nanoparticles to PGS/PCL polymeric scaffolds caused both the surface area and roughness to increase culminating in an improved cells adhesion on the ternary scaffolds.

DAPI staining is a complementary study conducted here to evaluate the adhered cells on the scaffolds. The cell nucleus shows cellular states such as the arrangement and viability of cells on the fibrous scaffolds [14,15]. Fig. 14 shows the micrographs taken through fluorescence microscopy showing the nuclei of cells on the scaffolds. It is visible that the myoblast cells were formed along the fiber's direction. The results showed that G2P1Q0.5 scaffold had better functionality and fiber arrangement for cells adhesion and proliferation than PCL and PGS/PCL scaffolds.

The cell viability of scaffolds— P, PQ0.5, G2P1, G1P1Q1, G2P1Q0.5, and G2P1Q1—were assessed through MTT assay up to 7 days. Through this assay (Fig. 15 ), it was attempted to examine the effects of PGS and CQDs on the viability of rats' myoblast cells. The results illustrated that G2P1 and G2P1Q0.5 scaffolds led to an acceptable cell proliferation up to 7 days (close to the negative control). However, increasing in the CQDs contents up to 1 wt % caused the cell viability to decrease dramatically for both G1P1Q1 and G2P1Q1 scaffolds; the high amounts of CQDs were to blame. The results of cell viability study complied that the CQDs threshold above which a decrease in the ternary scaffolds’ viability occurs is 0.5 wt %.

In this study, a new multifunctional scaffold composed of PGS/PCL/CQDs was designed and fabricated through electrospinning technique for cardiac muscle regeneration. Different amounts of PGS and CQDs were added to assess their effects on the physical, chemical, and biological properties of PCL scaffold. It was found that both PGS and CQDs caused the PCL fibers diameter to decrease dramatically and also an aligned fiber structure was achieved. The addition of both PGS and CQDs led to an increase in the wettability of PCL scaffolds significantly and the effects of PGS were much stronger than CQDs. The existence and distribution of CQDs were proved through fluorescence assay and TEM. The PGS/PCL scaffolds containing CQDs were found to be electrically conductive which can be beneficial in the case of cardiac tissue engineering. Raising the CQDs amounts up to 1 wt % was synchronized with a decrease in the cells viability. Therefore, the PGS/PCL/CQDs scaffold (2:1:0.5) was chosen as the optimum sample with a promising potential for cardiac muscle regeneration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the scientific support for this work by the University of Isfahan and Isfahan University of Medical Sciences.
